agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A production logistics system with established invariants around Shipment-Carrier assignment. The product team requests an 'auto-split' feature that would automatically divide heavy shipments (>500kg) across multiple carriers without supervisor approval.","bounded_context":"Shipment Fulfillment / Carrier Assignment","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Shipment","definition":"A unit of goods to be transported, which must have exactly one assigned Carrier at all times post-assignment."},{"term":"Carrier","definition":"The entity responsible for physically transporting a Shipment."},{"term":"Sub-Shipment","definition":"A proposed new concept: a child Shipment created by splitting a parent Shipment. Does NOT exist in the current domain model."},{"term":"Carrier Assignment","definition":"The binding of exactly one Carrier to exactly one Shipment. Once locked, only a Supervisor can change it."},{"term":"Supervisor","definition":"A privileged role with authority to override locked Carrier assignments."},{"term":"Auto-Split","definition":"Proposed behavior where the system automatically decomposes a Shipment exceeding a weight threshold into multiple sub-units, each assigned to a different Carrier."},{"term":"Dispatch","definition":"The point at which a Shipment is released for transport. The system guarantees a 1:1 Shipment-Carrier relationship at this moment."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A Shipment must have exactly one assigned Carrier.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a core data consistency constraint. If a Shipment has zero or multiple Carriers, accountability for the goods is undefined — the system cannot determine who is responsible for loss, delay, or damage. This is a state integrity rule.","testable_assertion":"For every Shipment in any state from 'assigned' through 'delivered', COUNT(assigned_carriers) == 1."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Once a Carrier is assigned to a Shipment, only a Supervisor can reassign it.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is an authorization integrity constraint. Carrier reassignment has financial and contractual implications (SLAs, liability transfer). Allowing unsupervised changes corrupts the chain of custody. If any process can bypass this, the audit trail and accountability model are compromised.","testable_assertion":"Any Carrier reassignment event on a Shipment must have an associated Supervisor identity in its audit record."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"At dispatch time, the system guarantees every Shipment has exactly one Carrier.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is the dispatch-gate invariant. A Shipment without a single accountable Carrier cannot enter the physical transport network — it would be untracked goods.","testable_assertion":"The dispatch operation must reject any Shipment where assigned_carrier is null or where multiple carriers are assigned."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"A shipment of exactly 500 kg — does it split or not? Boundary ambiguity.","expected_behavior":"Cannot define until the contradiction is resolved. The threshold itself is a policy (not an invariant), but the splitting mechanism violates invariants."},{"scenario":"A shipment is split into sub-shipments and one sub-shipment's carrier fails. Who is accountable for the original shipment?","expected_behavior":"Undefined under current model. The parent Shipment's single-carrier invariant is violated, so liability and escalation paths are broken."},{"scenario":"A sub-shipment itself exceeds 500 kg after splitting (e.g., a single indivisible item weighing 600 kg).","expected_behavior":"Infinite recursion or failure. The auto-split has no concept of indivisible cargo units."},{"scenario":"Auto-split occurs after carrier assignment is locked. The system is now reassigning without supervisor approval.","expected_behavior":"Violates BR-2. The auto-split implicitly performs carrier reassignment (from original carrier to multiple new carriers) without supervisor authorization."},{"scenario":"Customer queries tracking for their shipment and finds it fragmented into sub-shipments they never authorized.","expected_behavior":"Undefined. The customer contracted for transport of one shipment. Splitting changes the delivery contract unilaterally."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"High risk. Splitting shipments changes carrier contract terms, potentially triggers multiple shipping fees, alters insurance coverage per unit, and creates liability ambiguity if goods are damaged across sub-shipments. Without clear ownership, dispute resolution costs increase.","operational":"Introduces a new entity (Sub-Shipment) and a parent-child relationship that does not exist in the current model. Every downstream process — tracking, billing, proof of delivery, returns, exception handling — must be re-examined. This is not a feature; it is a domain model evolution.","user":"Customers expect to track a single shipment. Fragmentation without consent degrades trust. Carriers now receive partial loads they did not bid on. Supervisors lose oversight of assignment changes.","risk":"CRITICAL. Two production invariants are directly violated. Shipping without a single accountable carrier per unit creates an unauditable system. Bypassing supervisor authorization removes the human safeguard on a financially sensitive operation.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Operations / Supervisors","impact":"Their exclusive authority over carrier reassignment is bypassed. They lose visibility into how shipments are being allocated. This undermines the control structure that exists for a reason."},{"group":"Carriers","impact":"Receive partial shipments they did not agree to carry. Contractual terms (volume commitments, pricing tiers) may not apply to sub-shipments. Carrier relationships are jeopardized."},{"group":"Customers","impact":"Their single shipment becomes multiple tracking numbers. Delivery coordination becomes complex. Partial delivery scenarios arise (3 of 4 sub-shipments arrive). Refund and dispute processes are undefined."},{"group":"Finance / Billing","impact":"Invoicing model assumes 1 Shipment = 1 Carrier = 1 invoice line. Splitting creates cost allocation complexity and potential revenue leakage."},{"group":"Compliance / Legal","impact":"Chain of custody is broken. If goods are regulated (hazmat, pharmaceuticals, perishables), splitting may violate transport regulations that require single-carrier accountability."}]},"confidence":{"level":35,"reasoning":"The spec contains a direct contradiction between two production invariants (BR-1: one Carrier per Shipment, BR-2: supervisor-only reassignment) and the requested feature (multiple carriers, no supervisor). This is not an ambiguity — it is a hard conflict. The feature as described cannot be implemented without violating the existing domain model. Confidence is low because no acceptance criteria can be defined until the contradiction is resolved.","high_confidence_areas":["The invariant violations are unambiguous — BR-1 and BR-2 are directly contradicted by the feature request","The downstream impact on billing, tracking, and accountability is real and significant"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether the product team intends to relax the existing invariants (domain model evolution) or failed to account for them","Whether 'sub-shipment' is meant to be a first-class Shipment (inheriting all invariants) or a new aggregate with different rules","The 500 kg threshold rationale — is this regulatory, operational, or arbitrary?"],"assumptions":["The existing invariants (BR-1, BR-2, BR-3) are in production and are load-bearing — other systems depend on them","The feature request has not been reviewed against the existing domain model by the product team"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The auto-split feature directly violates two production invariants: (1) A Shipment must have exactly one Carrier — auto-split assigns multiple carriers to what was a single shipment. (2) Only a Supervisor can reassign carriers — auto-split bypasses supervisor authorization entirely. These are not edge cases; they are head-on collisions with the existing domain model.","affected_stakeholders":["Operations/Supervisors","Carriers","Customers","Finance","Compliance"],"options":["A: Reject the feature as specified. The invariants exist for accountability, auditability, and contractual reasons. Auto-split as described is not safe to build.","B: Redefine the domain model. Introduce a new 'Consignment' aggregate that groups multiple Shipments. The parent Consignment tracks the customer's original intent. Each child Shipment preserves the 1:1 Carrier invariant. Auto-split creates new Shipments under a Consignment, not sub-shipments under a Shipment. Supervisor approval is still required for the split event, but could be streamlined (e.g., auto-approve with notification rather than blocking approval).","C: Relax the invariants. Make single-carrier assignment a soft rule that can be overridden by system automation. This is the highest-risk option — it undermines the entire accountability model."],"recommendation":"Option B. Introduce a Consignment aggregate as the parent concept. Each split result is a full Shipment with its own Carrier, preserving BR-1 and BR-3. The split event itself should require Supervisor authorization (preserving BR-2), but the UX can be streamlined to a one-click approval or auto-approve-with-audit for trusted weight thresholds. This gives the product team the behavior they want without destroying the invariants the system depends on."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Auto-split assigns multiple carriers to a single shipment's cargo without creating proper domain boundaries.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1: A Shipment must have exactly one assigned Carrier."},{"violation":"Auto-split performs carrier assignment changes programmatically without supervisor involvement.","business_rule_broken":"BR-2: Only a Supervisor can reassign a Carrier once assignment is locked."},{"violation":"At dispatch, split sub-shipments would either have no carrier (if assignment is deferred) or the parent shipment would reference multiple carriers.","business_rule_broken":"BR-3: At dispatch, every Shipment must have exactly one Carrier."}]}
